news_summary: Discoveries of blood vessels in T. rex bones are transforming our understanding of dinosaur biology and evolution.
news_summary_fr: La découverte de vaisseaux sanguins dans les os du T. rex est en train de révolutionner notre compréhension de la biologie et de l'évolution des dinosaures.
news_summary_es: Los hallazgos de vasos sanguíneos en los huesos del T. rex están transformando nuestra comprensión de la biología y la evolución de los dinosaurios.

articles.title: Blood vessels found in T. rex bones are rewriting dinosaur science
articles.summary: Dinosaur DNA may still be out of reach, but scientists are uncovering something almost as exciting—ancient blood vessels hidden inside fossilized bones. In a massive Tyrannosaurus rex nicknamed Scotty, researchers discovered a network of preserved vessels within a rib that once fractured and began healing 66 million years ago. Using powerful synchrotron X-rays from particle accelerators, they were able to peer inside the dense fossil without damaging it, revealing intricate, iron-rich structures left behind by the healing process.
articles.content:
Despite decades of effort, scientists have never recovered dinosaur DNA. Most paleontology research today still focuses on searching for traces of original organic material in fossils, but DNA has not survived the passage of time.

Much of what we understand about dinosaurs comes from fossilized bones and teeth. These durable remains preserve well, but they offer only limited insight into how these animals actually lived.

Soft tissues, on the other hand, can reveal far more. These rare fossilized materials include muscles and ligaments, pigments or even skin (like scales or feathers). They provide important clues about appearance, movement, and behavior.

Another type of soft tissue sometimes preserved inside bones is blood vessels. My research team and I identified preserved blood vessels in a Tyrannosaurus rex fossil, and our findings were recently published in Scientific Reports.

A Discovery That Began With Physics

As an undergraduate physics student at the University of Regina, I joined a research group that used particle accelerators to study fossils. During that time, I used advanced 3D imaging techniques to examine a T. rex bone and noticed structures that appeared to be blood vessels.

Nearly six years later, I am now pursuing a PhD, continuing to apply physics-based methods to improve how fossils are analyzed.

The Largest T. Rex Ever Found

The preserved vessels came from an extraordinary specimen known as Scotty. Housed at the Royal Saskatchewan Museum in Canada, Scotty is the largest T. rex ever discovered and one of the most complete.

Evidence suggests Scotty lived a difficult life around 66 million years ago. Many of its bones show signs of injury, possibly from combat with another dinosaur or from disease. One rib stands out, showing a large fracture that had only partially healed.

When bones are damaged, the body increases blood vessel activity in the affected area to support healing. The structures we observed in Scotty's rib appear to be part of that process, forming a dense network of mineralized vessels that we reconstructed using 3D models.

Advanced Imaging Reveals Hidden Structures

Studying the inside of fossil bones presents two major challenges. First, researchers need to look inside without damaging the specimen. Second, fossilized bones are extremely dense because minerals have replaced the original organic material over millions of years.

We initially considered using an computed topography (CT) scan, similar to those used in medicine. While this method is non-destructive, standard CT scanners cannot penetrate the dense structure of large fossils.

Instead, we turned to synchrotron light, a powerful form of high-intensity x-rays produced at specialized particle accelerator facilities. This technique allowed us to visualize tiny internal features such as blood vessels with remarkable clarity.

Synchrotron imaging also made it possible to analyze the chemical composition of the structures. The vessels had been preserved as iron-rich mineralized casts, which is a common fossilization process. Interestingly, they appeared in two distinct layers, reflecting a complex environmental history that contributed to their preservation.

What Blood Vessels Reveal About Dinosaur Life

The partially healed fracture in Scotty's rib offers a rare opportunity to study how a T. rex recovered from injury. By examining the preserved blood vessels, researchers can gain insight into healing processes and survival strategies in large predatory dinosaurs.

This work may also provide a basis for comparison with other dinosaur species and with modern animals such as birds, which are closely related to dinosaurs.

The findings could guide future fossil discoveries as well. Bones that show signs of injury or disease may be more likely to preserve blood vessels or other soft tissues, helping scientists target promising specimens.

With the combination of physics, paleontology, and advanced imaging technologies, researchers are beginning to uncover details about dinosaur biology that were once thought impossible to study.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific discovery using advanced imaging technology to reveal preserved blood vessels in a T. rex fossil, providing new insights into dinosaur biology and healing processes. This breakthrough has broad implications for paleontology and showcases the impactful application of physics and technology to uncover previously inaccessible information about ancient life.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find preserved blood vessels inside T. rex bones using advanced X-rays
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied dinosaur bones to learn about these ancient animals, but soft tissues like blood vessels rarely survive fossilization. DNA has never been found in dinosaur fossils, so researchers look for other clues inside bones to understand dinosaur biology.

**What happened** – A team of scientists used powerful synchrotron X-rays, which come from particle accelerators, to scan the rib bone of a famous Tyrannosaurus rex named Scotty. Scotty is the largest and one of the most complete T. rex fossils found. The rib showed a healed fracture from 66 million years ago. The scans revealed a network of preserved blood vessels inside the bone, mineralized with iron, which formed during the healing process.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that soft tissues like blood vessels can survive in fossils much longer than previously thought. It helps scientists learn how dinosaurs healed injuries and lived their lives. Using this method, researchers can study dinosaur biology in more detail and compare it with modern animals like birds, which are related to dinosaurs.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to use these advanced imaging techniques on other fossils, especially those showing signs of injury or disease, to find more preserved soft tissues. This could change how paleontologists select fossils to study and lead to new discoveries about dinosaur health and biology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using advanced X-rays, scientists have uncovered preserved blood vessels inside a T. rex bone, opening new ways to study dinosaur biology and healing.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des vaisseaux sanguins découverts dans les os du T. rex grâce à des rayons X avancés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps les os de dinosaures pour mieux comprendre ces animaux anciens, mais les tissus mous comme les vaisseaux sanguins se conservent rarement dans les fossiles. L’ADN des dinosaures n’a jamais été retrouvé, donc les chercheurs cherchent d’autres indices à l’intérieur des os.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de scientifiques a utilisé des rayons X puissants appelés synchrotrons, produits par des accélérateurs de particules, pour scanner une côte d’un célèbre Tyrannosaure rex nommé Scotty. Scotty est le plus grand et l’un des fossiles de T. rex les plus complets découverts. La côte montrait une fracture en cours de guérison vieille de 66 millions d’années. Les images ont révélé un réseau de vaisseaux sanguins préservés à l’intérieur de l’os, minéralisés avec du fer, formés pendant la guérison.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que les tissus mous comme les vaisseaux sanguins peuvent survivre dans les fossiles bien plus longtemps qu’on ne le pensait. Cela aide les scientifiques à comprendre comment les dinosaures guérissaient de leurs blessures et vivaient. Cette méthode permet d’étudier la biologie des dinosaures en détail et de la comparer avec des animaux modernes comme les oiseaux, leurs proches parents.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’utiliser ces techniques d’imagerie avancée sur d’autres fossiles, surtout ceux montrant des blessures ou maladies, pour trouver plus de tissus mous préservés. Cela pourrait changer la façon dont les paléontologues choisissent les fossiles à étudier et mener à de nouvelles découvertes sur la santé et la biologie des dinosaures.

**Phrase clé** – Grâce à des rayons X avancés, les scientifiques ont découvert des vaisseaux sanguins dans un os de T. rex, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour étudier la biologie et la guérison des dinosaures.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran vasos sanguíneos preservados dentro de huesos de T. rex usando rayos X avanzados
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado huesos de dinosaurios durante mucho tiempo para aprender sobre estos animales antiguos, pero los tejidos blandos como los vasos sanguíneos rara vez se conservan en los fósiles. Nunca se ha encontrado ADN de dinosaurio, por lo que los investigadores buscan otras pistas dentro de los huesos.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de científicos usó rayos X potentes llamados sincrotrones, que provienen de aceleradores de partículas, para escanear una costilla de un famoso Tyrannosaurus rex llamado Scotty. Scotty es el T. rex más grande y uno de los fósiles más completos encontrados. La costilla mostraba una fractura en proceso de curación de hace 66 millones de años. Las imágenes revelaron una red de vasos sanguíneos preservados dentro del hueso, mineralizados con hierro, formados durante la curación.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que tejidos blandos como los vasos sanguíneos pueden sobrevivir en fósiles mucho más tiempo de lo que se pensaba. Ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo los dinosaurios sanaban sus heridas y vivían. Este método permite estudiar la biología de los dinosaurios con más detalle y compararla con animales modernos como las aves, que están relacionadas con los dinosaurios.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar estas técnicas avanzadas de imagen en otros fósiles, especialmente aquellos que muestran señales de lesiones o enfermedades, para encontrar más tejidos blandos preservados. Esto podría cambiar cómo los paleontólogos eligen fósiles para estudiar y llevar a nuevos descubrimientos sobre la salud y biología de los dinosaurios.

**Frase clave** – Usando rayos X avanzados, los científicos han descubierto vasos sanguíneos preservados dentro de un hueso de T. rex, abriendo nuevas formas de estudiar la biología y curación de los dinosaurios.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a massive T. rex skeleton partially embedded in earth, with glowing, intricate red and gold networks of mineralized blood vessels artistically flowing through one fractured rib, illuminated softly by beams of ethereal synchrotron light streaming from an abstract particle accelerator in the background.
